definition: Inspiring awe or reverence; worthy of profound respect; majestic.
confidence_reason: Attested in Old English religious usage with positive connotation of divine majesty.
citation: OED 2nd ed., awful adj. historical senses.
reconstruction_level: attested

definition: Causing great fear or dread; terrifying; dreadful.
confidence_reason: Documented in Middle English with strong negative emotional force.
citation: Middle English Dictionary, awful adj.
reconstruction_level: Shift from positive awe to fearful dread.

definition: Very bad or unpleasant; of poor quality.
confidence_reason: Widely attested in nineteenth-century colloquial usage.
citation: OED 2nd ed., awful adj. colloquial sense.
reconstruction_level: Loss of original intensity through semantic bleaching.

definition: Foolish; wanton; lascivious; dissolute.
confidence_reason: Earliest English sense is pejorative and historically tied to Latin nescius through Old French.
citation: Middle English Dictionary, nice adj.
reconstruction_level: Origin: Latin nescius via Old French nice (foolish).

definition: Precise; careful; fastidious; requiring exactness.
confidence_reason: Intermediate stage between foolish and pleasant; used in technical and scholarly contexts.
citation: OED 2nd ed., nice adj. precise/exacting senses.
reconstruction_level: attested

definition: Pleasant; agreeable; kind; socially acceptable.
confidence_reason: Now the dominant sense in most registers.
citation: OED 2nd ed., nice adj. pleasant sense.
reconstruction_level: One of the most documented cases of amelioration in English.

definition: To come before; to precede; to anticipate.
confidence_reason: The original English sense follows Latin praevenire as priority rather than obstruction.
citation: Middle English Dictionary, prevenen v.
reconstruction_level: Calque of Latin praevenire. Now an opaque etymological sense.

definition: To hinder or obstruct; to keep from happening.
confidence_reason: The semantic shift from preceding to obstructing is complete in Early Modern English.
citation: OED 2nd ed., prevent v. hinder sense.

definition: To stop something from happening; to avert.
confidence_reason: The universal modern sense is well documented in modern dictionaries and historical dictionaries.
citation: OED 2nd ed., prevent v. modern sense.

definition: To carry; to bear (a burden or load).
confidence_reason: Latin portare is an etymological source form, not an attested English period meaning.
citation: Lewis and Short, portare.
reconstruction_level: Root of import export transport portable: all trace to Latin portare.

definition: Having nothing; poor; needy; worthless.
confidence_reason: From naught (nothing) plus -y. Original sense concerned poverty or worthlessness rather than mild misbehavior.
citation: Middle English Dictionary, noughti adj.
reconstruction_level: Striking pejoration: poverty to moral badness.

definition: Wicked; morally bad; evil.
confidence_reason: The moral sense displaces the poverty sense in Early Modern English.
citation: OED 2nd ed., naughty adj. wicked sense.

definition: Mildly disobedient; playfully bad; mischievous.
confidence_reason: Further bleaching from wicked to merely mischievous.
citation: OED 2nd ed., naughty adj. mild/mischievous sense.
reconstruction_level: Classic three-stage drift: poverty to evil to mischievous.

definition: Blessed; happy; spiritually fortunate.
confidence_reason: From Old English saelig (blessed); cognate with German selig.
citation: OED 2nd ed., silly adj. early blessed/happy senses.
reconstruction_level: attested

definition: Innocent; helpless; pitiable; deserving of compassion.
confidence_reason: Shift from blessed to innocent remains positive or neutral.
citation: Middle English Dictionary, sely adj.

definition: Weak-minded; simple; feeble.
confidence_reason: Connotation turns negative as helplessness becomes foolishness.
citation: OED 2nd ed., silly adj. simple/weak senses.

definition: Foolish; absurd; lacking good sense.
confidence_reason: The modern universal sense is well documented.
citation: OED 2nd ed., silly adj. foolish sense.
reconstruction_level: Complete journey from blessed to foolish in about five centuries.

definition: Physical or spiritual co-suffering; feeling another's pain as one's own.
confidence_reason: Wycliffe-era usage preserves the bodily and spiritual force of co-suffering rather than abstract sympathy.
citation: Wycliffe Bible (1382), Matthew 9:36.
reconstruction_level: Earlier compassion implies shared suffering more concretely than modern sympathy.

definition: Suffering alongside another; empathetic pain that still carries bodily force.
confidence_reason: KJV usage still frames compassion as being moved inwardly by another's distress.
citation: King James Bible (1611), Matthew 9:36.
reconstruction_level: The physical dimension weakens but remains present.

definition: Warm emotional sympathy and concern for another's suffering.
confidence_reason: Modern usage broadens into a largely emotional and ethical response rather than shared bodily suffering.
citation: OED 2nd ed., compassion n. modern sense.
reconstruction_level: Modern compassion keeps care but loses most of the literal co-suffering force.

definition: One who bears suffering with endurance or calmness; one acted upon or afflicted.
confidence_reason: Early Modern English still centers suffering and endurance rather than medicine alone.
citation: Shakespeare, 'Be patient' usages; early modern literary sense.
reconstruction_level: attested

definition: A person under a physician's care or receiving medical treatment.
confidence_reason: The endurance sense narrows institutionally into the medical context that now dominates the noun.
citation: OED 2nd ed., patient n. medical sense.
reconstruction_level: The older moral sense survives mainly in the adjective.

definition: The suffering and death of Jesus Christ; sacred ordeal and torment.
confidence_reason: Middle English religious usage strongly centers the Passion as Christ's suffering rather than emotion.
citation: Niermeyer, Medieval Latin Dictionary, passio / Passion of Christ usage.
reconstruction_level: The original English center of gravity is sacred suffering.

definition: Any overwhelming emotion that seizes or controls a person: grief, rage, love, or fear.
confidence_reason: Early Modern English extends passion from suffering into overpowering emotion while retaining passivity before the feeling.
citation: Shakespeare, 'What passion hangs these weights upon my tongue?'
reconstruction_level: You are the sufferer of the emotion rather than its master.

definition: Intense romantic or sexual feeling; strong enthusiasm for a person or activity.
confidence_reason: Modern usage narrows and domesticates the older field of overwhelming suffering and emotion.
citation: OED 2nd ed., passion n. modern emotional sense.
reconstruction_level: The suffering sense largely survives only in religious or formal contexts.

definition: To hinder, obstruct, delay, or hold back.
confidence_reason: Old English lettan is the older semantic opposite of the dominant modern verb.
citation: Hall 1894, lettan.
reconstruction_level: attested

definition: To hinder or prevent; to hold someone back from action.
confidence_reason: Early Modern biblical usage preserves the older obstructive sense clearly and memorably.
citation: King James Bible (1611), Romans 1:13.
reconstruction_level: The older sense survives in fixed legal phrasing like 'without let or hindrance.'

definition: To allow or permit; to grant leave for something to happen.
confidence_reason: The dominant modern meaning fully reverses the inherited sense of hindrance.
citation: OED 2nd ed., let v. allow sense.
reconstruction_level: One of the clearest semantic reversals in common English.

definition: Manner of life; conduct; social behavior; the way one carries oneself.
confidence_reason: KJV usage shows conversation meaning conduct rather than spoken exchange.
citation: King James Bible (1611), Philippians 1:27.
reconstruction_level: A major false friend for readers of older religious prose.

definition: Spoken exchange between people; verbal social interaction.
confidence_reason: The moral-conduct sense narrows into verbal exchange
evidence_grade: which becomes the default modern meaning.
citation: B
page: OED 2nd ed., conversation n. spoken exchange sense.
learner_level: The older sense survives only in specialized historical reading.

definition: Christian love; selfless love of God and neighbor; agape.
confidence_reason: KJV famously preserves charity as love rather than almsgiving in 1 Corinthians 13.
citation: King James Bible (1611), 1 Corinthians 13:1.
reconstruction_level: Modern readers often miss the theological sense entirely.

definition: Benevolent giving to the poor; almsgiving; organized relief.
confidence_reason: The theological sense narrows toward practical benevolence and public giving.
citation: OED 2nd ed., charity n. benevolence/almsgiving sense.
reconstruction_level: Religious love becomes social relief.

definition: An organization that helps those in need, or the act of donating to one.
confidence_reason: The institutional sense now dominates everyday usage more than the theological one.
citation: OED 2nd ed., charity n. organization/donation sense.
reconstruction_level: The word now often points to institutions before virtues.

definition: Alive; living; possessing life.
confidence_reason: Old English cwic means living rather than rapid and survives fossilized in a few expressions.
citation: Hall 1894, cwic.
reconstruction_level: attested

definition: Alive and active; living with force or vitality.
confidence_reason: The KJV preserves quick as alive rather than speedy in one of the most famous false-friend passages.
citation: King James Bible (1611), Hebrews 4:12.
reconstruction_level: Older readers hear vitality where modern readers hear speed.

definition: Fast; rapid; moving or happening with speed.
confidence_reason: The vitality sense fades and speed becomes the dominant modern meaning.
citation: OED 2nd ed., quick adj. rapid sense.
reconstruction_level: The older living sense remains in 'the quick and the dead' and 'quicken'.

definition: Honorable, respectable, chaste, or of good social standing.
confidence_reason: Early Modern honest often points to moral reputation or sexual virtue
citation: A
page: Shakespeare, Othello, 'honest Iago' and related uses.

definition: Truthful; not lying, cheating, or deceiving.
confidence_reason: The modern core meaning narrows from social honor and chastity to verbal and ethical truthfulness.
citation: OED 2nd ed., honest adj. truthful sense.
reconstruction_level: A broad status term becomes a narrower moral one.

definition: Foolish, simple-minded, or doting.
confidence_reason: Middle English fond remains closely tied to folly and simple-mindedness.
citation: Middle English Dictionary, fond adj.
reconstruction_level: The affectionate modern sense would have sounded semantically strange at first.

definition: Foolishly in love; dotingly attached; naive through attachment.
confidence_reason: Early Modern fond still often means foolishly attached rather than merely affectionate.
citation: Shakespeare, 'I am too fond' and related uses.
reconstruction_level: The pejorative edge is still audible here.

definition: Affectionate; having liking or tenderness toward someone or something.
confidence_reason: The older foolishness weakens and affection becomes the dominant interpretation.
citation: OED 2nd ed., fond adj. affectionate sense.
reconstruction_level: A former insult becomes an ordinary positive adjective.

definition: A house of hospitality for pilgrims, travelers, the poor, and the sick.
confidence_reason: Middle English hospital still names a broad house of reception rather than a specialized medical institution.
citation: Middle English Dictionary, hospital n.
reconstruction_level: Hospital once emphasized hospitality as much as medicine.

definition: An institution increasingly centered on the care and treatment of the sick.
confidence_reason: The care-of-strangers function narrows toward medical specialization in the modern period.
citation: OED 2nd ed., hospital n. medical institution sense.
reconstruction_level: The reception function gives way to clinical care.

definition: A medical institution providing diagnosis, treatment, nursing, and emergency care.
confidence_reason: The medical sense now dominates so fully that the hospitality history is mostly invisible.
citation: OED 2nd ed., hospital n. modern sense.
reconstruction_level: Its earlier link to host and hospitality is no longer obvious to most readers.

definition: Divine favor, aid, or beneficence; a gift or sign of God's goodwill.
confidence_reason: Middle English grace is strongly theological and often names divine aid rather than social elegance.
citation: Middle English Dictionary, grace n.
reconstruction_level: The religious sense is older and deeper than the modern social ones.

definition: God's saving favor or unearned divine assistance.
confidence_reason: KJV usage preserves grace as a central theological term rather than merely charm or courtesy.
citation: King James Bible (1611), Ephesians 2:8.
reconstruction_level: A key false friend in religious reading because modern readers often hear only elegance or niceness.

definition: Elegance, pleasing ease, courteous beauty, or favor shown by one person to another.
confidence_reason: Modern common usage often foregrounds elegance or social favor rather than divine aid.
citation: OED 2nd ed., grace n. elegance/favor senses.
reconstruction_level: The word branches rather than simply narrows in modern English.

definition: Mercy, clemency, compassion, tenderness, or charitable fellow-feeling.
confidence_reason: Middle English pity overlaps strongly with mercy
citation: and affection rather than condescension.
column30: The emotional range is broader and warmer than in many modern uses.

definition: Sympathetic sorrow for suffering, often with a sense of superiority or distance.
confidence_reason: Modern pity is often narrower and more condescending than the older word's range of mercy and tenderness.
citation: OED 2nd ed., pity n. modern sympathetic sorrow sense.
reconstruction_level: Modern pity can sound cooler or more hierarchical than older pity.

definition: Physical strength, force, power, vigor, or efficacy.
confidence_reason: Middle English virtue can mean power or force
citation: B
page: Middle English Dictionary, vertu n. physical strength/power senses.
learner_level: Older readers may miss the energetic or effective sense entirely.

definition: Power, efficacy, or force working within a person or thing; also moral excellence.
confidence_reason: Early Modern English still allows virtue to mean active power or efficacy as well as moral goodness.
citation: King James Bible (1611), Mark 5:30.
reconstruction_level: This sense survives faintly in older religious phrasing but is no longer primary.

definition: Moral excellence; good character; a morally admirable quality.
confidence_reason: Modern usage strongly privileges moral goodness over force or efficacy.
citation: OED 2nd ed., virtue n. moral excellence sense.
reconstruction_level: The moral sense becomes dominant and crowds out the older energetic one.

definition: To endure, undergo, bear, or experience pain, grief, or hardship.
confidence_reason: The core older sense is to bear or undergo rather than to permit.
citation: Middle English Dictionary, suffren v.
reconstruction_level: The endurance sense remains alive but is no longer the whole story.

definition: To allow, permit, or let something happen or someone do something.
confidence_reason: Early Modern biblical English frequently uses suffer where modern readers would expect allow or permit.
citation: King James Bible (1611), Matthew 19:14.
reconstruction_level: A major older-reading trap because the permissive sense is now rare or archaic.

definition: To endure pain, loss, difficulty, or harm; to be afflicted.
confidence_reason: Modern English preserves the endurance sense while largely losing the old permissive one outside formal or biblical style.
citation: OED 2nd ed., suffer v. endure sense.
reconstruction_level: The word's surviving main sense is only one branch of its earlier range.

definition: A position of duty, service, responsibility, employment, or authority; often ecclesiastical or administrative.
confidence_reason: Middle English office centers duty and role more than physical workspace.
citation: Middle English Dictionary, office n.
reconstruction_level: The institutional role sense is older than the room sense.

definition: A room, department, or workplace used for professional or administrative work.
confidence_reason: Modern everyday usage often points first to a place rather than a duty or charge.
citation: OED 2nd ed., office n. workplace/room sense.
reconstruction_level: The place takes over from the responsibility as the default image.

definition: Mercy, clemency, leniency, or compassion shown toward another person.
confidence_reason: Middle English mercy strongly overlaps with clemency
citation: and divine compassion rather than mild politeness alone.
column30: The word is warmer and more theological than many modern uses suggest.

definition: Divine or human clemency; compassionate restraint from harm or punishment.
confidence_reason: Early Modern biblical English keeps mercy close to divine clemency and saving compassion.
citation: King James Bible (1611), Psalm 23:6.
reconstruction_level: Mercy in older prose often carries spiritual weight that modern casual uses do not.

definition: Compassion, forbearance, or leniency, especially toward someone in one's power.
confidence_reason: Modern usage preserves compassion and leniency
evidence_grade: but usually with less overt theological charge.
citation: B
page: OED 2nd ed., mercy n. modern sense.
column30: but with reduced sacred intensity in everyday use.

definition: Innocent, guileless, unsophisticated, plain, or lacking complexity or learning.
confidence_reason: Middle English simple can be neutral or positive where modern readers may hear only deficiency.
citation: Middle English Dictionary, simple adj.
reconstruction_level: The older word often names innocence or plainness before it names stupidity.

definition: Easy, plain, uncomplicated, or intellectually unsophisticated.
confidence_reason: Modern usage splits between neutral clarity and mildly pejorative lack of intelligence or sophistication.
citation: OED 2nd ed., simple adj. modern senses.
reconstruction_level: The innocent sense weakens while the deficiency reading grows more audible.

definition: Immediately, at once, or without delay.
confidence_reason: Early Modern presently usually means at once or very soon rather than merely at the present time.
citation: Shakespeare, Hamlet, presently.
reconstruction_level: attested

definition: At the present time; now, or in some varieties soon.
confidence_reason: Modern usage divides between now and soon
evidence_grade: with the older immediate sense weakened or restricted by variety and style.
citation: B
page: OED 2nd ed., presently adv. modern senses.
learner_level: A common adverb whose timing assumptions can mislead readers across periods.

definition: A peasant, serf, rustic laborer, or socially low rural person.
confidence_reason: The original social sense points to rank and rural status
citation: B
learner_level: A class label later hardens into a moral accusation.

definition: A wicked, cruel, or morally reprehensible person; the bad character in a story.
confidence_reason: Modern usage fully moralizes the word and largely erases its older social-class grounding.
citation: OED 2nd ed., villain n. wicked person sense.
reconstruction_level: One of the clearest class-to-morality drifts in common English.

definition: Pleasure, delight, desire, longing, or eager inclination.
confidence_reason: Old English lust is broad and not restricted to sexuality; it can name desire or pleasure generally.
citation: OED 2nd ed., lust n. early desire/pleasure senses.
reconstruction_level: attested

definition: Strong sexual desire; craving marked by sensual appetite.
confidence_reason: Modern English sharply narrows lust toward sexual desire
evidence_grade: making the older broad-desire sense largely invisible.
citation: B
page: OED 2nd ed., lust n. sexual desire sense.
learner_level: A once-broad desire word becomes morally and sexually specific.
